points: Palampur is a hypothetical village with 450 families; 80 upper caste families own most land while one-third of the population (dalits) are landless; the village has good infrastructure including all-weather roads, electricity, two primary schools, one high school, a government primary health centre, and a private dispensary.
The village is well-connected to neighbouring Raiganj (3 km away) and Shahpur town through various modes of transport—bullock carts, tongas, bogeys, motorcycles, jeeps, tractors, and trucks.

The four factors of production are: land (natural resources like water, forests, minerals), labour (workers ranging from highly educated to manual workers), physical capital (fixed capital like tools and machines; working capital like raw materials and money), and human capital (knowledge and enterprise to combine resources).
Fixed capital (plough, tractors, generators) is used over many years; working capital (seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, cash) is used up in production.
Farming employs 75% of workers; land area is fixed with no expansion since 1960. Production is increased through multiple cropping (2-3 crops per year) and modern farming methods.
Cropping pattern includes kharif (jowar, bajra), potato (October-December), rabi (wheat), and sugarcane; irrigation through electric tubewells covers the entire 200 hectares.
The Green Revolution (late 1960s) introduced HYV seeds that increased wheat yield from 1300 kg/hectare to 3200 kg/hectare but required chemical fertilizers, pesticides, and more water.
Modern farming has led to environmental concerns—loss of soil fertility from chemical fertilizers and depletion of groundwater from excessive tubewell use.
Land distribution is highly unequal: 150 families (one-third) are landless; 240 families cultivate small plots (<2 hectares); 60 families are medium/large farmers (>2 hectares).

Small farmers use family labour and struggle to make a living; medium/large farmers hire farm labourers like Dala and Ramkali who earn below minimum wage (Rs 160 vs government Rs 300) due to heavy competition.
Small farmers like Savita borrow at high interest (24%) and face harsh loan conditions; large farmers like Tejpal Singh have savings from surplus production and can finance farming themselves.

Only 25% of workers are engaged in non-farm activities: dairy (milk sold through collection centres), small-scale manufacturing (Mishrilal's jaggery production using mechanical crusher), shopkeeping (general stores, Kareem's computer classes), and transport.

Kishora's story illustrates how a landless labourer improved his livelihood through a bank loan under a government scheme—he bought a buffalo for milk and transport business, earning more than before.
Non-farm activities require little land but need capital; availability of markets in nearby towns (Raiganj, Shahpur) and good roads facilitate their growth.
Land is fixed and scarce; labour is abundant; capital is scarce especially for small farmers who must borrow, while large farmers accumulate capital from surplus.
Small farmers have little surplus, struggle to repay loans, and often work as labourers; large farmers have substantial surplus, save, lend, and invest in fixed capital.
Farming provides main livelihood but limited employment; non-farm activities offer alternative income sources but are currently limited to 25% of workers.
